title: The efficacy of new non-invasive brain stimulation in patients with chronic tinnitus without specific treatable origin: a systematic review and network meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
abstract:
Background/Objectives: Despite the high prevalence (around 4.1–37.2%) and highly debilitating adverse impact, there has been inconclusive evidence regarding the efficacy of treatment for tinnitus management, especially for those patients with tinnitus who do not have a specific or treatable origin. The aim of this updated network meta-analysis (NMA) was to estimate the efficacy and safety of the different non-invasive brain stimulation (NIBS) interventions in tinnitus management in patients with chronic tinnitus without a specific or treatable origin.

Methods: This NMA included randomized controlled trials (RCTs) of NIBS interventions in patients with chronic tinnitus. The current NMA was conducted using the frequentist model. The primary outcome was a change in tinnitus severity after the NIBS intervention.

Results: We identified 45 eligible RCTs with a total of 2042 participants. The results of the current NMA showed that both excitatory and inhibitory NIBS interventions exerted significant effects on tinnitus severity, quality of life, or response rate. While several protocols showed a signal, the top-ranked intervention was preliminary and derived from a single, small study. All the NIBS interventions had fair acceptability compared to the controls.

Conclusions: This NMA highlighted that both excitatory and inhibitory NIBS interventions exerted significant effects on tinnitus severity, quality of life, and/or response rate. Future well-designed RCTs with electroencephalogram applications are needed for replication over the proclamation of efficacy.

Abstract
Background/Objectives: Despite the high prevalence (around 4.1–37.2%) and highly de-
bilitating adverse impact, there has been inconclusive evidence regarding the efficacy of
treatment for tinnitus management, especially for those patients with tinnitus who do not
have a specific or treatable origin. The aim of this updated network meta-analysis (NMA)
was to estimate the efficacy and safety of the different non-invasive brain stimulation (NIBS)
interventions in tinnitus management in patients with chronic tinnitus without a specific or
treatable origin. Methods: This NMA included randomized controlled trials (RCTs) of NIBS
interventions in patients with chronic tinnitus. The current NMA was conducted using the
frequentist model. The primary outcome was a change in tinnitus severity after the NIBS
intervention. Results: We identified 45 eligible RCTs with a total of 2042 participants. The
results of the current NMA showed that both excitatory and inhibitory NIBS interventions
exerted significant effects on tinnitus severity, quality of life, or response rate. While several
protocols showed a signal, the top-ranked intervention was preliminary and derived from
a single, small study. All the NIBS interventions had fair acceptability compared to the
controls. Conclusions: This NMA highlighted that both excitatory and inhibitory NIBS
interventions exerted significant effects on tinnitus severity, quality of life, and/or response
rate. Future well-designed RCTs with electroencephalogram applications are needed for
replication over the proclamation of efficacy.
Keywords: tinnitus; repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation; transcranial direct current
stimulation; non-invasive brain stimulation; network meta-analysis; systematic review
1. Background
The chronic tinnitus imposed a highly debilitating adverse impact on about 6–25%
of affected people [1]. Several diseases might be associated with tinnitus, such as dys-
lipidaemia, atherosclerosis [2], and lower hemoglobin and platelet levels [3]. Although
there have been some consensus guidelines [4,5] or large-scale network meta-analyses
(NMAs) [6,7] addressing the efficacy and acceptability of different treatment strategies for
tinnitus, the treatment efficacy has still not achieved a satisfactory state for patients with
tinnitus. The insufficient treatment efficacy might result from the diverse recognition of the
tinnitus physiopathology model. Specifically, there were two distinct theories regarding
tinnitus physiopathology. The first one mainly focused on the pathological changes in the
hearing system, including the cochlea (or inner ear), vestibulocochlear nerve, and nerve
conduction [8–12], and recommended systematic pharmacologic treatments [6]. The other
one, based the Jastreboff Neurophysiological Model [13], investigated the limbic system
(including the amygdala, anterior cingulate cortex, and insula), frontal cortical areas, and
consequent autonomic nervous system alterations [7].
The tinnitus was a complex disease with various systemic diseases involved. For
example, in cases of persistent tinnitus without a specific treatable origin, the researchers
had noticed a strong association between tinnitus and cardiovascular and metabolic condi-
tions (ex. dyslipidemia and atherosclerosis), which indicated microvascular dysfunction,
inflammation, and oxidative stress, in a recent comprehensive analysis by Maihoub et al. [2].
Furthermore, in another important report investigating physiological markers, the authors
noticed that hematological parameters (lower hemoglobin and platelet levels) were asso-
ciated with increased tinnitus severity and chronicity [3]. All these findings pointed out
the potential role of oxygen delivery and inflammatory pathways in tinnitus perception,
so that tinnitus might not be a simple otologic symptom but a manifestation of systemic
https://doi.org/10.3390/ohbm7010007
J. Otorhinolaryngol. Hear. Balance Med. 2026, 7, 7
3 of 23
dysregulation, thereby providing a strong rationale for investigating treatments like non-
invasive brain stimulation (NIBS) that target central nervous system networks influenced
by these peripheral conditions.
In chronic tinnitus without a specific or treatable origin, abnormal hyperactivity in
specific brain areas such as the bilateral auditory cortices [14], anterior cingulate cortex [15],
and insula [15] has been demonstrated in several clinical trials. The NIBS targeting the indi-
cated brain regions has, therefore, been proposed as a tinnitus management strategy [16].
The NIBS techniques vary widely based on their mechanism and include repetitive tran-
scranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) [17], rTMS variant theta-burst stimulation (TBS) [18],
transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) [19], and transcranial random noise stimula-
tion (tRNS) [20]. These different NIBS mechanisms exert different effects on the targeted
brain regions [18] and result in different efficacy on tinnitus severity. In our previous
large-scale NMA [7], we summarized the currently available NIBS interventions applied to
the management of tinnitus without a specific or treatable origin. In our previous NMA,
we noticed that NIBS with an inhibitory effect on the auditory cortex was associated with
significant efficacy in relieving tinnitus severity [7]. However, in this rising research field,
there have been several important and new NIBS strategies for tinnitus management. Most
of these studies focused on excitatory NIBS modalities and found a preferable efficacy for
tinnitus severity, for example, neuronavigated NIBS [21], tDCS with high current [22], high
definition tDCS [23], high-frequency rTMS over dorsomedial prefrontal cortex [24], and
assistance of tailor-made notched music training [25]. These new excitatory NIBS strategies
showed promising efficacy on tinnitus severity [22,25]. Therefore, to provide up-to-date
information and evidence for clinicians, an updated state-of-the-art NMA is required.
Therefore, we conducted an updated NMA of the currently published RCTs to estimate
the efficacy and safety of the different NIBS interventions in tinnitus management in
patients with tinnitus who do not have a specific or treatable origin.
2. Materials and Methods
2.1. General Guidelines of the Study
The present NMA follows the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews
and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) 2020 guidelines (Table S1) and AMSTAR2 (assessing the
methodological quality of systematic reviews) guidelines. This study was registered in
PROSPERO (CRD42022381048).
2.2. Search Strategy and Selection Criteria
We conducted an updated systematic review using PubMed, Cochrane CENTRAL,
ClinicalKey, ProQuest, Embase, ScienceDirect, Web of Science, and ClinicalTrials.gov to
identify potentially eligible articles. The initial search date was 2 December 2022, and the
final update search was performed on 25 September 2025. No language restrictions were
applied. The detailed search strategies and keywords used in each database are listed in
Table S2. Additionally, manual searches were performed for potentially eligible articles
selected from the reference lists of the review articles and pairwise meta-analyses.
2.3. Inclusion and Exclusion Criteria
Following the rationale of our previous large network meta-analyses (NMAs) [6,7], the
PICO applied in the current updated NMA was as follows: (1) participants—patients with
chronic tinnitus who do not have a specific or treatable origin; (2) intervention—central
NIBS intervention; (3) comparison—sham control or active control or waiting list; and
(4) outcomes—changes in tinnitus severity. The definition of “tinnitus without specific or
treatable origin” followed the definition of primary tinnitus addressed in the guidelines by
https://doi.org/10.3390/ohbm7010007
J. Otorhinolaryngol. Hear. Balance Med. 2026, 7, 7
4 of 23
Tunkel et al. (2014) [5]. The non-invasive brain stimulation (NIBS) methods were repetitive
transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS), transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS),
transcranial random noise stimulation (tRNS), continuous TBS (cTBS), and intermittent
TBS (iTBS). To improve the overall quality of the current NMA, we only included peer-
reviewed randomized controlled trials (RCTs). Therefore, the inclusion criteria were as
follows: (1) RCTs published in peer-reviewed journals; (2) patients with tinnitus who do
not have a specific or treatable origin; (3) use of one or more central NIBS interventions as
the experimental group; and (4) human trials.
The exclusion criteria were as follows: (1) not a clinical trial, (2) not an RCT, (3) not
reporting the target outcomes (defined ahead), and (4) not related to the NIBS methods
mentioned earlier. In cases of duplicate use of data (i.e., different articles based on the same
sample sources), we included only the article with the largest sample size.
2.4. Data Extraction
Two authors independently screened and extracted relevant data from the included
studies. If the original data were not available, the original corresponding authors were
electronically contacted via email to request the original data. Discrepancies were adjudi-
cated by a third author. We only extracted data from the NIBS and did not include data on
peripheral stimulation.
2.5. Outcome Definition
The primary outcome was the change in tinnitus severity after NIBS intervention in
patients with tinnitus without a specific or treatable origin.
The secondary outcomes were changes in quality of life and the response rate related
to the NIBS method in patients with tinnitus without a specific or treatable origin. The
response rate was defined based on the criteria used in the included studies. Finally,
acceptability was defined using the dropout rate, which was defined as the percentage of
patients who left early studies for any reason.
2.6. Node Definition
As addressed before, the previous meta-analyses addressing the efficacy of various
NIBSs in tinnitus categories (i.e., rTMS versus control or tDCS versus control) lead to wide
confidence intervals and significant heterogeneity. Therefore, we decided to specifically
separate the various NIBSs based on stimulating sites. As the NIBS method varied widely
among the studies, we categorized the methods into five major subgroups: (i) rTMS
modalities, which are high-frequency rTMS (HF-rTMS) (>1 Hz) and low-frequency rTMS
(LF-rTMS) (≤1 Hz); (ii) deep TMS (dTMS) modalities; (iii) TBS modalities, including
iTBS and cTBS; (iv) tDCS modalities, which were specifically categorized according to
the anodal/cathodal placement position (for example, a-tDCS over F3 or c-tDCS over
F3); and (v) a combination of tDCS and other specific interventions, such as tRNS. We
further categorized the treatment arms based on the stimulation position according to
the electroencephalogram (EEG) brain map following the rationale of classification in our
previous NMA of NIBS in different diseases [7].
2.7. Cochrane Risk of Bias Tool
Two independent authors evaluated the risk of bias (inter-rater reliability, kappa = 0.89)
for each domain using the Cochrane risk of bias tool. Discrepancies were resolved by con-
sulting a third author.

2.8. Statistical Analysis
NMA was performed using the STATA statistical software (version 16.0; StataCorp
LLC, College Station, TX, USA). For continuous variables, we used the standardized mean
difference (SMD) with 95% confidence intervals (95% CIs) as the effect size measure. For
categorical data, the effect size measure was the odds ratio (OR) with 95% CIs. Although
there had not been direct evidence regarding the interpretation of SMD values of THI,
we chose the ordinary reports regarding SMD value interpretation in general conditions.
Specifically, the SMD value of 0.2 indicated a small effect, 0.5 for medium, and 0.8 for
large, respectively [26]. When no events were observed in one of the treatment groups, we
applied the continuity correction method by adding 0.5 events to both arms. However, if
both the intervention and control arms had no events in a study, this correction procedure
was not applied owing to the increased risk of bias. We used the frequentist random effect
model for NMA [27]. All comparisons were performed using a two-tailed t-test, and a
p-value of <0.05 was considered statistically significant. Heterogeneity among the included
studies was evaluated using the tau value, which is the estimated standard deviation of the
effects across the included studies.
Regarding the meta-analysis procedure employed in this study, we used generalized
linear mixed models to undertake direct and indirect comparisons [28]. Specifically, indirect
comparisons were conducted under the assumption of transitivity, in which the differences
between treatments A and B could be calculated from their comparisons with a third
treatment C [29]. STATA was used in our NMA with the mvmeta command and self-
programmed STATA [27]. The restricted maximum likelihood method was used for the
estimation of NMA [30].
To enhance the clinical application of our results, we used the ranking probabilities of
treatments compared with the analysis of each outcome to calculate the surface under the
cumulative ranking curve (SUCRA). The SUCRA, ranging from 0 to 100, is the percentage
of the effect of each treatment relative to an imaginary treatment, which is always the best
with absolute certainty [31].
Based on the rationale of the transitivity assumption test used in our previous NMA
studies [32], we assessed the variations in the effects of sham or control interventions
across different trials to evaluate the validity of the transitivity assumption. Specifically,
we computed the sham therapy effect for tDCS sham therapy, rTMS sham therapy, and
waiting list by the traditional pairwise meta-analysis using Comprehensive Meta-Analysis
(version 3; Biostat, Englewood, NJ, USA) [33]. Finally, we evaluated the potential inconsis-
tencies between the direct and indirect evidence within the network using the loop-specific
approach and identified local inconsistencies using the node-splitting method. The design-
by-treatment interaction model was used to evaluate global inconsistencies within the
entire NMA [34]. We used the comparison-adjusted funnel plots [35] and Egger regression
to evaluate potentially small study effects and publication bias within the direct evidence on
the comparison between a pair of treatments. In addition, we evaluated the heterogeneity
of tau values in the main results of this study. To evaluate the potential placebo effect of
sham applications in the RCT with a sham control design, we arranged a subgroup analysis
focusing on RCTs with a sham control design.
3. Results
After the initial screening procedure, 142 articles were considered for the full-text
review (Figure 1). However, 97 were excluded for various reasons (Table S3). Finally, after
adding 13 new RCTs to our previous NMA, 45 articles were included in the current study
(Table S4). Figure 2 depicts the entire geometric distribution of the treatment arms.

3.1. Characteristics of the Included Studies
A total of 2042 participants were included in the study. The mean age of the par-
ticipants was 50.2 years (range = 40.0–60.55 years), and the mean proportion of females
was 35.4% (range = 0.0–79.7%). The mean duration of NIBS treatment was 15.1 weeks
(range, 1–54 weeks). The baseline characteristics of the participants are summarized in
Table S4. The definition of response varied between the recruited studies, with patient
self-rated global impression, tinnitus handicap inventory (THI) score reduction > 7, 10, or
20 points, THI score reduction > 20% of the baseline THI, and tinnitus questionnaire (TQ)
score reduction > 5 or 10 points.
3.2. Primary Outcome: Change in Tinnitus Severity
The NMA revealed that only the a-tDCS-T3 + c-tDCS-F4 (SMD = −2.55, 95% CIs = −3.86
to −1.25), a-tDCS-F4 + c-tDCS-F3 + tRNS-T3 (SMD = −1.97, 95% CIs = −2.98 to −0.96),
a-tDCS-F4 + c-tDCS-F3 + TMNMT (SMD = −1.67, 95% CIs = −2.80 to −0.54), HF-rTMS-
Fp1Fp2 (SMD = −1.34, 95% CIs = −2.40 to −0.29), cTBS-T3T4 (SMD = −0.72, 95%
CIs = −1.41 to −0.04), HF-rTMS-F3 + LF-rTMS-T3T4 (SMD = −0.71, 95% CIs = −1.32
to −0.10), and LF-rTMS-T3 (SMD = −0.30, 95% CIs = −0.55 to −0.06) were associated with
significant improvement in the severity of tinnitus compared to the controls (Tables 1 and 2,
and Figure 3). The main results would not be changed after removing single-study nodes.
According to the SUCRA, a-tDCS-T3 + c-tDCS-F4 was associated with the largest improve-
ment, followed by a-tDCS-F4 + c-tDCS-F3 + tRNS-T3, and a-tDCS-F4 + c-tDCS-F3+ TMNMT
(Table S5A). The transitivity assumption test was verified using the interaction test. There
was no significant difference in placebo effects between the rTMS sham therapy, tDCS sham
therapy, and waiting list (p = 0.355; Figure S2A). However, a significant placebo effect was
detected in the rTMS sham controls (SMD = −0.152, 95% CIs: −0.300 to −0.004, p = 0.045).

3.3. Primary Outcome: Change in Tinnitus Severity in the Subgroup of Sham-Controlled Trials
In the subgroup analysis of sham-controlled RCTs, the main result of the NMA did
not change. To be specific, only the a-tDCS-T3 + c-tDCS-F4 (SMD = −2.55, 95% CIs = −3.79
to −1.32), a-tDCS-F4 + c-tDCS-F3 + TMNMT (SMD = −1.67, 95% CIs = −2.72 to −0.63),
HF-rTMS-Fp1Fp2 (SMD = −1.34, 95% CIs = −2.31 to −0.38), HF-rTMS-F3 + LF-rTMS-T3T4
(SMD = −0.71, 95% CIs = −1.25 to −0.17), HF-rTMS-T3 (SMD = −0.50, 95% CIs = −0.92
to −0.07), LF-rTMS-T3T4 (SMD = −0.40, 95% CIs = −0.79 to −0.01), and LF-rTMS-T3
(SMD = −0.29, 95% CIs = −0.51 to −0.07) were associated with significant improvement in
the severity of tinnitus compared to the controls (Table S6A, Figures S1A and S2B). Accord-
ing to the SUCRA, a-tDCS-T3 + c-tDCS-F4 was associated with the largest improvement,
followed by a-tDCS-F4 + c-tDCS-F3+ TMNMT, and HF-rTMS-Fp1Fp2 (Table S5B).
3.4. Secondary Outcome: Change in Quality of Life
The NMA revealed that only the a-tDCS-T3 + c-tDCS-F4 (SMD = −2.45, 95%
CIs = −3.63 to −1.28), a-tDCS-F4 + c-tDCS-F3 + TMNMT (SMD = −1.41, 95% CIs = −2.38
to −0.44), a-tDCS-F4 + c-tDCS-F3 + tRNS-T3 (SMD = −1.28, 95% CIs = −2.11 to −0.46),
HF-rTMS-Fp1Fp2 (SMD = −1.01, 95% CIs = −1.90 to −0.13), LF-rTMS-F3 + LF-rTMS-T3
(SMD = −0.83, 95% CIs = −1.41 to −0.25), LF-rTMS-T3T4 (SMD = −0.51, 95% CIs = −0.87
to −0.16), HF-rTMS-T3 (SMD = −0.44, 95% CIs = −0.84 to −0.05), and LF-rTMS-T3
(SMD = −0.34, 95% CIs = −0.56 to −0.11) were associated with significant improve-
ments in the quality of life in patients with tinnitus compared to the controls (Table S6B,
Figures S1B and S2C). According to the SUCRA, a-tDCS-T3 + c-tDCS-F4 was associated
with the largest improvement in quality of life, followed by a-tDCS-F4 + c-tDCS-F3+ TM-
NMT, and a-tDCS-F4 + c-tDCS-F3 + tRNS-T3 (Table S5C).
3.5. Secondary Outcome: Response Rate
The NMA revealed that only HF-rTMS-T3 (OR = 6.68, 95% CIs = 1.74 to 25.58), LF-
rTMS-F3 + LF-rTMS-T3 (OR = 5.16, 95% CIs = 1.88 to 14.16), and LF-rTMS-T3T4 (OR = 4.59,
95% CIs = 1.24 to 17.00) were associated with significantly better response rates than
the controls (Table S6C, Figures S1C and S2D). According to SUCRA, HF-rTMS-Fp1Fp2
(OR = 15.19, 95% CIs = 0.69 to 333.18 compared to controls) was associated with the highest

response rate, followed by HF-rTMS-T3. However, the result of HF-rTMS-Fp1Fp2, which
had wide confidence intervals, indicated an extreme imprecision so that the result might be
less reliable and needs future research to replicate it (Table S5D).
3.6. Acceptability in the Dropout Rate
In the NMA, none of the investigated NIBSs were associated with significantly different
dropout rates compared with the controls (Tables S5E and S6D, Figures S1D and S2E).
3.7. Risk of Bias, Funnel Plot, Heterogeneity, and Publication Bias
We found that 61.6% (194/315 items), 30.8% (97/315 items), and 7.6% (24/315 items) of
the included studies had low, unclear, and high risks of bias, respectively. Unclear reporting
of allocation concealment or blindness of the studies further contributed to the risk of bias
(Figure S3A,B).
Funnel plots of publication bias (Figure S4A–J) revealed general symmetry, and the
Egger’s test results indicated no significant publication bias among the articles included
in the NMA. In general, the NMA did not demonstrate inconsistencies in terms of either
local inconsistencies, as assessed using the loop-specific approach and node-splitting
method, or global inconsistencies, as determined using the design-by-treatment method
(Tables S7 and S8). In addition, according to the I2 and τ2 in the heterogeneity test, there
was no significant heterogeneity in the main results of this study (detailed I2 and τ2 value
please see Table S9). The GRADE ratings revealed the overall quality of evidence to be low
to medium (Table S10).
4. Discussion
In contrast to our previous NMA, which mainly addressed inhibitory NIBS efficacy on
tinnitus management [7], this updated NMA found that both excitatory (i.e., high current
a-tDCS-T3 + c-tDCS-F4, high current a-tDCS-F4 + c-tDCS-F3 + TMNMT, HF-rTMS-Fp1Fp2,
and high current a-tDCS-F4 + c-tDCS-F3 + tRNS-T3) and inhibitory NIBS (i.e., cTBS-T3T4,
HF-rTMS-F3 + LF-rTMS-T3T4, and LF-rTMS-T3) exerted significant treatment effects on
tinnitus severity, quality of life, and the response rate. Further, a-tDCS-T3 + c-tDCS-F4
was associated with the largest improvement in both tinnitus severity and quality of life
among all NIBS treatments investigated. However, before applying our results in clinical
practice, the clinicians should pay special attention due to the predominance of single-
study nodes, small sample sizes, and heterogeneity in protocols and outcome measures
among the recruited RCTs in this NMA. To date, tDCS has been found to have divergent
brain-acting effects depending on its polarity. Specifically, the excitatory or inhibitory
effect of tDCS might rely on its current-dependent effect but not polarization-dependent
effect [36–38]. The evidence of the efficacy of a-tDCS-T3 + c-tDCS-F4 in the current NMA
mainly came from one RCT by Souza et al. (2020) [22], in which the intensity of the
current was 2 mA, so it fell within the excitatory effect range. In that previous RCT, the
authors also investigated EEG before and after tDCS application and found that tDCS
application modulated the theta band in the limbic system and beta band in the temporal
regions, which were associated with the tinnitus component (i.e., attention and perception).
However, because the efficacy of a-tDCS-T3 + c-tDCS-F4 mainly came from one RCT [22],
the evidence of a-tDCS-T3 + c-tDCS-F4 in tinnitus management remains preliminary, so
clinicians should be especially careful when applying this finding in their clinical practice.
Therefore, the current NMA cannot definitively recommend one protocol over another for
clinical practice due to this evidence’s fragility.
Another remarkable finding of the current NMA was that among these NIBS
interventions with statistically significant efficacy, both excitatory (i.e., high current

a-tDCS-T3 + c-tDCS-F4, high current a-tDCS-F4 + c-tDCS-F3 + TMNMT, HF-rTMS-Fp1Fp2,
and high current a-tDCS-F4 + c-tDCS-F3 + tRNS-T3) and inhibitory NIBS (i.e., cTBS-T3T4,
HF-rTMS-F3 + LF-rTMS-T3T4, and LF-rTMS-T3) exerted significant treatment effects on
tinnitus severity, quality of life, and the response rate. These conflicting findings might
not be consistent with the previous findings regarding the hyper-excitability in the cortical
regions in patients with tinnitus. Rather, there has recently been another newly devel-
oped hypothesis regarding the varied efficacy of NIBS [39]. The “neural noise hypothesis”
suggested that the after-effect of NIBS might mainly rely on the overall altered activity
of the dopaminergic, GABAergic, glutamatergic, and serotonergic synapses through the
network axon projection connection within the whole brain [40]. Central gain was another
important physiopathology based on the neural noise hypothesis, which signals were
the main target of brain stimulation [7]. As addressed in Qi’s report [41], abnormally
enhanced bidirectional EEG activities across multiple timescales and frequency bands had
been found in tinnitus patients. Similar findings could also be found in the report by Shen
and colleagues [42], in which the authors found a complex reorganization in the brain EEG
activities in idiopathic tinnitus patients. Therefore, the improvement in tinnitus severity,
quality of life, or response rate might not be simply determined by the depolarization or
hyperpolarization in different cortices related to the after-effect of specific NIBS on specific
brain regions. Rather, we should consider the whole brain network [40] and acknowledge
that excitatory or inhibitory NIBS interventions could both contribute to a rectified effect
on the whole brain function so that it might achieve a more steady state after the NIBS
interventions [22]. The rectification theory was mainly based on the desynchronized neural
conduction in the brain region and led to tinnitus sound formation [43]. The theory stated
that rectifying the desynchronized signals in the brain would theoretically manage (or
reduce) tinnitus severity [44]. Therefore, needs for further multi-center, well-designed RCTs
with EEG applications was still strong to replicate the findings of promising single-study
protocols (like a-tDCS-T3 + c-tDCS-F4). (The discussion regarding the placebo effect was
depicted in Table S11).
Several potential limitations should be considered in the current NMA. First, this
NMA may have been underpowered due to the heterogeneity of the participants (e.g.,
comorbidities, physiological measures, baseline severity of tinnitus, duration of tinnitus
onset, commercial machine used in each study, and follow-up duration), variety in the
definition of response, and variety in tinnitus severity or quality of life rating scales. Those
varieties might not be simply dealt with statistics measurement and might impose potential
fundamental bias on the result of this NMA. Since there had not been a consistent consensus
on the definition of chronic tinnitus across countries [45], the wide variation in tinnitus
duration might imply potential bias for the result of this study. Although there was no
statistically significant heterogeneity or inconsistency, some differences were observed
between the included RCTs, which could be attributed to other unknown factors. Despite
our effort to include a large number of RCTs (i.e., 45 RCTs), the variability in stimulation
parameters, patient demographics, and tinnitus characteristics across studies might limit
the interpretation of this study. Specifically, these factors significantly influence the pooling
and interpretation of results. Second, although most RCTs included a sham control in
their study design, the blinding of those RCTs may not have been complete because of
the limitations of the commercial machine employed. Third, because there have been
some studies with small sample sizes and short follow-up duration, there might be some
potential small outliers within the included RCTs. The efficacy during long term follow-up
should be interpreted with caution. To investigate this issue, we evaluated the funnel plot
and found no significant outliers within the included RCTs. Further, the main results of
our study remained similar after removing single-study nodes. However, the efficacy of

a-tDCS-T3 + c-tDCS-F4 mainly came from one RCT with small sample sizes (n = 27) [22].
Specifically, the SUCRA, which ranked treatments based on the position within the network
comparison, might be adversely affected by a single trial with extensive efficacy values.
Therefore, the evidence of this treatment arm should be interpreted with caution. Another
potential limitation of this study should be addressed. Specifically, the wide variety in
rating scales of tinnitus severity had been applied among the recruited RCTs. Although
the cut point of “clinically meaningful changes in THI” had been defined [46], this was
not applicable to most other rating scales. Therefore, the statistical significance might
not always reflect the clinical significance. Although we used response rate to reflect
clinically relevant improvement, the various definitions of response rate might still impose
potential bias on the results of our study. In addition, to reduce potential heterogeneity
among the treatment arms, we only included NIBS in the current study and excluded other
treatment options, such as pharmacologic treatments and auditory training/sound therapy.
Although this selection would increase homogeneity and consistency, it would make us
miss the comparison with other treatment options. Furthermore, although we extensively
divide various NIBSs into separate treatment arms to provide the most comprehensive
information, this process would lead to the consequently small sample sizes in some
treatment arms. Finally, the concerns of the transitivity assumption among this NMA
might need to be addressed. However, this might not be required because the transitivity
assumption test in the current NMA revealed no significantly different placebo effects
between the rTMS sham therapy, tDCS sham therapy, and waiting list. Additionally, some
RCTs lacked complete documentation of ethical approval and consent. Therefore, future
research with comprehensive reporting of ethical approval and consent procedures to
enhance transparency should be warranted.
5. Conclusions
The excitatory and inhibitory NIBS both exerted significant treatment effects on tin-
nitus severity, quality of life, and the response rate. All the NIBS interventions had fair
acceptability compared to the controls. Future well-designed RCTs with EEG applications
are warranted to explore the role of NIBS in tinnitus management.
